narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 93% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try, leading to the tough decision to reject your application. This prediction hinges on how your overall academic trajectory stacks up against patterns seen in students who succeed. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model a dip in performance that often foreshadows bar struggles. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 28—much lower than the typical 36.7—strongly reinforced this concern, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with first-time bar failures in the dataset. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average around 5.7, added further weight in the same direction, painting a picture of consistently lower class standings compared to peers who pass. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA pushed back toward a passing prediction, helping to soften the outlook somewhat. Your middle family income bracket nudged slightly toward failure risk, while planning to attend full-time—common for 93% of students—offered a small positive lift. Overall, these elements, especially your deciles and LSAT, placed you outside the profile of typical successful bar takers, who tend to rank higher in school and score stronger on entrance exams. While this stings, especially given the unique barriers Black women like you often face in competitive fields, it's not the end of your journey. We encourage you to retake the LSAT for a higher score, seek targeted academic support to boost your class rankings, and consider programs offering bar prep tailored to your background. With focused improvements, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger—many have turned similar predictions around. Keep pushing; your potential shines through.
